title: Everything is an object in Python

overview.what:
In Python, every value is an object — an entity with a type, a unique identity, and attributes. This includes things other languages treat as 'not values': functions, classes, and modules are all objects too.

Three consequences you can see directly:

1. Everything has a type, and types are themselves objects: type(42) is int, and type(int) is type.

  print(type(42))     # <class 'int'>
  print(type(int))    # <class 'type'>

2. Functions are objects, so they have attributes and can even carry custom ones:

  def handler(): pass
  print(handler.__name__)   # 'handler'
  handler.route = '/health' # attach a custom attribute

3. Anything that is an object can be stored in a variable, put in a list, or passed as an argument — including functions and classes:

  registry = [int, str, handler]

Think of Python as a world where there are no 'primitives' standing outside the object system — even an integer is a full object with methods (42 .bit_length()). The uniform rule 'it's an object' is what makes introspection (type(), getattr, dir) work on everything.
overview.why: This uniformity is why Python can pass functions as arguments, build registries of classes, and introspect any value at runtime. Frameworks like FastAPI rely on it: a route decorator receives your function as an object and stores it; dependency injection stores classes in a registry. Understanding it explains why function.attribute, type(x), and storing a class in a dict all just work.
where_used: FastAPI route/dependency registries (functions and classes stored as objects)
Plugin systems that map names to classes
Runtime introspection with type() and getattr()

why_learning_this: Explains how FastAPI stores your route functions and dependency classes in registries — they are objects
Enables introspection: using type(), getattr(), and attaching metadata to functions

common_mistakes.title: Thinking ints and strings are 'primitives' without methods
common_mistakes.explanation: Unlike Java, Python has no primitive types — (42).bit_length() and 'hi'.upper() work because int and str are full objects. There is no separate boxed/unboxed distinction.

common_mistakes.title: Calling a function object when you meant to reference it
common_mistakes.explanation: Storing handler() in a registry stores its return value; storing handler stores the function object itself. Omit the parentheses to keep the object.

common_mistakes.title: Assuming classes cannot be passed around
common_mistakes.explanation: A class is an object (an instance of type), so it can be stored in a variable, put in a list, or passed to a function — that is how factory and plugin patterns work.

practice_tasks.title: Build a Handler Registry
practice_tasks.prompt: Modify the registry logic so that the `registry` dictionary correctly stores the function objects for `/health` and `/users` rather than calling them.
practice_tasks.starter_code:
def health(): return 'ok'
def users(): return 'user list'

# TODO: Fix the registry to store function objects, not their return values
registry = {
    '/health': health(),
    '/users': users()
}

# Test logic
# handler = registry['/users']
# print(handler())
practice_tasks.solution:
def health(): return 'ok'
def users(): return 'user list'

registry = {
    '/health': health,
    '/users': users
}

handler = registry['/users']
print(handler())

code_walkthrough.code:
def handler():
    return 'ok'

print(type(handler))
print(handler.__name__)
handler.route = '/health'
print(handler.route)
code_walkthrough.focus: handler is an object: it has a type (function), a built-in __name__ attribute, and you can even attach a brand-new custom attribute (route) to it.

aha_moment.code:
def ping():
    return 'pong'

ping.call_count = 0
ping.call_count += 1
print(ping.call_count)
aha_moment.prediction: Can you attach your own attribute (call_count) to a function and read it back?
aha_moment.common_guess: Error — functions can't hold custom attributes
aha_moment.why: A function is an object, so it has a writable attribute namespace just like any instance. ping.call_count = 0 stores state directly on the function object, and ping.call_count reads it back — printing 1.

challenge.title: Type-keyed serializer dispatch
challenge.prompt: Write serialize(value) that uses type(value) as a dict key to pick a serializer function. Map int -> a function returning 'num:<v>', str -> 'str:<v>', and bool -> 'bool:<v>'. Store the serializer functions as objects in the dict. Test with 42 and 'hello'. (Note: check bool before int is not required here since the keys are exact types.)

recall_questions.q: Are functions and classes considered objects in Python?
recall_questions.answer: Yes, everything in Python is an object, including functions and classes.

recall_questions.q: Can you attach custom attributes to a function in Python?
recall_questions.answer: Yes, because functions are objects, they have an attribute namespace.

understanding_checks.question: What will the following code output?
understanding_checks.why: Because functions are objects in Python, they have an attribute namespace where you can dynamically attach custom attributes.

understanding_checks.question: Why is it possible to put a class, like `int`, into a dictionary as a key or value?
understanding_checks.answer: Because classes themselves are objects in Python.
understanding_checks.why: In Python, everything is an object, including classes. They have their own type (`type`), identity, and can be passed around just like instances.
